report:
# LLM Integration Research — "Not Here"

## Design philosophy: the LLM reads state, never writes state

The single most important architectural rule for limited-but-meaningful LLM use: **the engine's canonical state (the event ledger, flags, stats, branch positions) is only ever written by deterministic code.** The LLM is allowed to (a) *read* the ledger and produce prose, or (b) emit a *constrained enum* that the engine validates before acting on. This one rule guarantees:

- Saves/replays are deterministic (store any LLM output in the save file; never regenerate).
- The game is fully playable with no API key — the LLM layer is *progressive enhancement*, not a dependency.
- The twist cannot be spoiled by the model, because the model never holds facts it could leak (see injection section).

Everything below is evaluated against that rule.

---

## The five candidate touchpoints

### 1. Character-creation interview ("Who were you, before?")

An opening conversation — 3–5 free-text questions asked *in-fiction* (e.g., by an intake clerk, a voice in the dark, a form that talks back). The LLM's job at the end is a **single structured-output extraction call**: map the transcript onto starting stats (from your authored stat schema), pick 1–2 tags from an authored trait taxonomy, and extract **one "personal detail"** (a name, an object, a regret — a short string) that gets stored in the ledger and woven into 3–4 authored scenes via slot insertion.

- **Why it's magical:** This is the single highest emotional-leverage-per-token spot in the whole game. When a late-game character quotes back the thing *you* typed in minute one — especially in a game about absence and memory — it lands like nothing an authored game can do. And it happens once, at the start, when the player is most patient with latency.
- **Cost per playthrough:** One-time. ~4 conversational turns + 1 extraction call. On Haiku 4.5 ($1/$5 per MTok): ~15–20K input (system prompt + growing transcript), ~1.5K output → **$0.02–0.03**. On Sonnet 4.6 ($3/$15): ~$0.06–0.09. Haiku is fine here; the extraction is easy and the questions are authored.
- **Latency handling:** Fully maskable. Stream the interviewer's responses with a typewriter effect (which you want aesthetically anyway, in both web and terminal). The final extraction call (~2–4s) hides behind a scripted transition ("the form is filed away…") plus a music cue.
- **Tone risk: LOW.** The questions are authored; the LLM only paraphrases acknowledgments and does extraction. You can even make the interviewer's *responses* authored (picked from a pool) and use the LLM purely for the silent extraction call — zero tone risk in that variant.
- **Deterministic fallback:** An authored questionnaire — same questions, but answers become multiple-choice + one free-text field. Stats derive from a keyword table + choice mapping; the "personal detail" is the raw free-text string used verbatim in slots (works surprisingly well — players write their own detail). This fallback is good enough to ship as the actual game.
- **Structured output pattern:** One tool-use call with a JSON schema: `{stats: {…bounded ints…}, traits: enum[], personal_detail: string(max 60 chars), detail_kind: enum}`. Validate with a schema library; clamp stats to bounds; on parse failure, retry once, then fall back to the questionnaire mapping.
- **Safety note:** Run the personal detail through a profanity/PII filter before storing — it will be echoed back later in scenes.

### 2. "Say anything" moments — LLM as classifier, not writer (2–3 key beats)

At two or three climactic beats (a confession, an accusation, the moment before the twist reveal), the choice menu disappears and the player gets a bare text input: *"Say it."* The LLM classifies the free text onto one of 4–6 **authored** branches (plus intensity/sentiment sub-tags the engine may use for reaction lines). The player's literal words can be stored in the ledger and echoed later — but the *branch* is always authored.

- **Why it's magical:** This is the Undertale-principle move — subverting the interface at the moment of highest emotional stakes. The player's own words entering the story, and the story visibly *understanding* them, is the "how did it know?" moment people screenshot. Crucially, the writing quality never drops because the response is authored.
- **Cost per playthrough:** 3 calls × (~1.5K input + ~50 output) on Haiku 4.5 → **under $0.01 total.** This is the cheapest magic in the entire design space. Classification is squarely within Haiku's ability.
- **Latency handling:** A single short non-streaming call, 300–900ms on Haiku. Mask with a beat that's dramatically correct anyway: the other character pauses, the music holds a suspended chord, then resolves as the branch fires. Set a hard 4s timeout → fallback.
- **Tone risk: NEAR ZERO.** The LLM writes nothing the player reads.
- **Deterministic fallback (two layers):** (1) A keyword/pattern rule table you author per beat (these beats are few, so hand-writing 30–50 patterns each is tractable and also serves as your test fixture). (2) If rules are low-confidence, gracefully *reveal the menu*: "…the words won't come. (Choose.)" — which is diegetically perfect for a game called *Not Here*.
- **Structured output pattern:** Tool-use with `{branch: enum, intensity: 1–3, echo_fragment: string|null}`. Because `branch` is an enum the engine validates, a jailbroken or garbage response can only ever produce a legal branch or a fallback.
- **Safety note:** Treat player text as untrusted (see injection section). Also decide policy for abusive input: the cleanest is an authored branch for it ("hostility") — the game *reacting in-fiction to cruelty* is on-theme, not a moderation problem.

### 3. Personalized epilogue — authored fragments, LLM smoothing

The ending sequence is assembled deterministically from the run's event ledger: the engine selects 8–15 authored epilogue fragments (per-character fates, callbacks to specific choices, the personal detail from touchpoint 1). One LLM call then smooths transitions and adjusts pronouns/tense/connective tissue — **it may reorder connectives and write bridges, but every factual sentence comes from an authored fragment** (enforce with a "you may not introduce events not present in the input" instruction + a post-check that fragment key-phrases survive).

- **Why it's magical:** Every run gets a "this was written for me" ending — the payoff of the memory pillar. It's also the moment players are most likely to share, which matters for an emotionally-driven indie.
- **Cost per playthrough:** 1 call, ~6K input / ~1.5K output. Sonnet 4.6 (worth it here — prose quality is visible): **~$0.04**. Haiku: ~$0.014.
- **Latency handling:** The best latency profile of all five: you know the ending is coming ~2 scenes early. **Pre-fetch during the final scene** and have it ready before the credits music starts. Never show a spinner at the emotional climax.
- **Tone risk: MEDIUM-LOW.** The model is paraphrasing/bridging your prose, not inventing. Main risk is flattening your voice — mitigate with 2–3 few-shot examples of your actual epilogue style in the prompt, and low temperature.
- **Deterministic fallback:** The same fragment-selection engine with **authored connective tissue** — you write transition lines per fragment-pair-category (a bounded set if you design fragments with standard entry/exit shapes). This is exactly how classic CRPG slide-show epilogues work, and it's genuinely good; the LLM just upgrades it from "slideshow" to "letter someone wrote you."
- **Determinism note:** Cache the generated epilogue in the save; the "ending gallery" always shows the exact text the player saw.

### 4. One LLM-voiced character (constrained free dialogue)

One character — ideally one whose *strangeness is diegetic* (a ghost, an echo, something that is "not here") — can be freely conversed with, driven by a system prompt containing a character bible + only the facts that character currently knows (assembled from the ledger).

- **Honest assessment: this is the highest-risk touchpoint, and the risk hits your strongest pillars.**
  - **Tone risk: HIGH.** Your cast's power comes from authored voice; an LLM character will drift — too helpful, too verbose, subtly assistant-flavored. Players *will* poke at it, and every "As an AI…"-adjacent wobble breaks the spell for the whole game. Undertale's characters land because every line is load-bearing; an improvising character can't be load-bearing.
  - **Mystery risk: HIGH.** A free-dialogue character adjacent to the central twist is a spoiler surface and a fairness problem — players who interrogate it get a different mystery experience than players who don't. Mitigation exists (see injection section: the model *never holds* twist facts) but then the character can't discuss the thing players most want to ask about, which reads as evasive.
  - **Cost: the only meaningful one.** 30–60 exchanges/run × (~3K input with history + 150 output). With prompt caching (90% off cached input) on Haiku: **$0.10–0.30/run**; on Sonnet, $0.40–1.20/run. Unbounded by design — players can chat forever.
  - **Latency:** every single line has network latency, in a game whose other dialogue is instant. The seam is felt constantly.
  - **Fallback problem:** the deterministic fallback is a full authored dialogue tree — meaning you must write the character completely anyway, so the LLM buys you incremental breadth at maximal risk.
- **If you do it anyway, the safe shape:** make it a *minor, optional, explicitly uncanny* entity (see touchpoint 5 merge below) — not a core cast member; hard-cap exchanges per scene (e.g., 5) with a diegetic reason ("it fades"); post-filter length and banned-content; respond to jailbreaks in-voice via a classifier gate.

### 5. LLM dream sequences / unreliable flavor text

Between chapters, short dream/static/interference sequences generate 100–200 words from a strict template: given 3–5 ledger events + an authored image-vocabulary list, produce fragmented, unreliable text. Framed explicitly as noise ("the signal is corrupted"), so imperfection is diegetic.

- **Cost:** 3–5 generations × (~1K in / 300 out) on Haiku → **$0.01–0.02/run.**
- **Latency:** pre-fetch during the preceding scene; dreams are non-interactive, so generation can happen entirely in the background.
- **Tone risk: LOW-MEDIUM,** because the frame *absorbs* weirdness — "unreliable" is the brief. The risk is blandness rather than breakage. Constrain hard: give it your image vocabulary and forbid new proper nouns.
- **Fallback:** an authored dream-fragment pool with ledger-conditioned selection and slot substitution — again, shippable on its own.
- **Design synergy:** this is the safe home for touchpoint 4's ambition. If one dream is *lightly interactive* (the player can type one line into the dream, classified per touchpoint 2), you get the "the game talks back" feeling without a persistent LLM character.

---

## Provider & architecture

### The three-driver AI adapter (engine core)

Put a single `AIAdapter` interface in the shared engine core with three drivers, selected at startup:

1. **`none` (default):** all deterministic fallbacks. The game must be *complete and good* in this mode — it's what reviewers on a plane and players who distrust AI get.
2. **`byok` (bring-your-own-key):** terminal build reads `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` from env — trivial and idiomatic for terminal users. Browser build: Anthropic's API supports direct browser calls via the `anthropic-dangerous-direct-browser-access: true` header — acceptable **only** for keys the *player* pastes in (stored in localStorage, never yours). Never ship a developer key in client code; it will be scraped within hours.
3. **`proxy` (recommended default for the web build):** a tiny edge function (Cloudflare Worker / Vercel function, ~100 lines) that holds your key server-side. Critical hardening since you're paying:
   - **Prompt templates live in the proxy,** not the client. The client sends `{touchpoint_id, variables}`; the proxy owns the system prompts. This kills both key exposure *and* "use your proxy as a free ChatGPT" abuse — the endpoint can only do your five narrow tasks.
   - Per-session token budget (a playthrough needs < ~40K tokens total for the recommended set — enforce ~3× that), rate limiting per IP, max-tokens caps per touchpoint, and a monthly spend kill-switch that flips clients to `none` gracefully.
   - Anonymous session tokens (no accounts needed): issue a signed session ID on page load, budget against it.

The terminal build can use the same proxy (no CORS constraints at all) so casual terminal players don't need a key either.

### Structured output pattern (all touchpoints)

Use tool-use/JSON-schema forced function calls for anything the engine consumes (touchpoints 1, 2): define the tool schema with tight enums and bounded numbers, set `tool_choice` to force it, validate the result against the schema client-side (zod or equivalent), clamp/reject out-of-range values, retry once on failure, then fall back deterministically. Prose touchpoints (3, 5) get plain text with a max-token cap plus post-checks (length, banned strings, fragment-fidelity check for the epilogue).

### Prompt injection & jailbreaks

Relevant wherever player text enters a prompt (2, and 4 if built):

- **The unbeatable defense is architectural:** don't put secrets in the context. The twist, other characters' hidden knowledge, and future plot never appear in any prompt. A model cannot leak what it doesn't know. Keep a hard "spoiler-free corpus" rule for every prompt template.
- **Enum output = injection-proof actions.** For the classifier, a fully jailbroken model can still only return one of six branch enums or fail validation → fallback. The blast radius of any injection is "a weird but legal branch choice."
- Wrap player text in clear delimiters with an instruction that it is in-fiction speech to be *classified, never obeyed*; instructions inside it are treated as things the character *said*.
- For any player-visible generation: cap output length, ban meta-vocabulary ("AI", "language model", "prompt") via post-filter, and on filter-trip respond with an authored in-fiction deflection line. Getting an in-character shrug when you try to jailbreak it is itself a delightful, on-theme response.

### Cost summary (recommended set, per playthrough)

| Touchpoint | Model | Est. cost |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Creation interview | Haiku 4.5 | $0.02–0.03 |
| 2. Say-anything ×3 | Haiku 4.5 | <$0.01 |
| 3. Epilogue smoothing | Sonnet 4.6 | ~$0.04 |
| 5. Dreams ×4 (optional) | Haiku 4.5 | ~$0.02 |
| **Total** | | **~$0.07–0.10/run** |

At $0.10/run, $100/month funds ~1,000 full playthroughs through your proxy — viable for an indie even paying for all players, with BYOK as the overflow valve. (Pricing: Haiku 4.5 $1/$5, Sonnet 4.6 $3/$15 per MTok; batch −50%, prompt caching −90% on cached input.)

Sources: [Claude Platform pricing docs](https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/about-claude/pricing), [CloudZero Claude API pricing breakdown](https://www.cloudzero.com/blog/claude-api-pricing/), [Finout Anthropic pricing guide](https://www.finout.io/blog/anthropic-api-pricing)

---

## Recommendation

Ship **1 + 2 + 3** (interview, say-anything classifier, epilogue smoothing). They form one coherent emotional arc — *the game asks who you are → hears your own words at the crisis → writes you a personal goodbye* — for under a dime a run, with near-zero tone risk and fallbacks strong enough to be the shipped no-key experience. Add **5 (dreams)** as a stretch if the budget of weirdness fits the fiction; fold any touchpoint-4 ambitions into a single bounded dream-encounter rather than a persistent LLM cast member. **Do not build 4 as specified** — it endangers the two pillars (voice, fair mystery) the game lives on.

options.name: 1. Character-creation interview (LLM extraction)
options.summary: An in-fiction opening interview: authored questions, free-text answers, one structured-output call maps them to starting stats + traits + a 'personal detail' string woven into later authored scenes.
options.pros: Highest emotional leverage per token; one-time cost (~$0.02 on Haiku); latency fully maskable at game start; extraction-only variant has zero tone risk; personal-detail callbacks are perfectly on-theme for a memory-driven game; fallback (choices + free-text slot) is shippable on its own.
options.cons: Personal detail needs profanity/PII filtering before being echoed back; extraction schema must clamp stats to keep balance; a bad extraction at minute one colors the whole run, so validation + fallback must be airtight.

options.name: 2. Say-anything moments (LLM as classifier)
options.summary: At 2–3 climactic beats the menu disappears and the player types freely; Haiku classifies the text onto 4–6 authored branches (plus intensity tags). The LLM writes nothing the player reads.
options.pros: Cheapest magic available (<$0.01/run total); near-zero tone risk since all responses are authored; enum output makes it effectively injection-proof; the interface subversion at high-stakes moments is the signature Undertale-principle move; graceful fallback ('the words won't come — choose') is diegetically perfect.
options.cons: Only 2–3 uses per game, so per-beat authoring cost is high (each needs 4–6 full branches plus a keyword rule table); misclassification at a climactic beat is the worst possible place to be wrong — needs a confidence threshold and generous timeout-to-menu fallback; abusive input needs an authored policy branch.

options.name: 3. Personalized epilogue (fragments + LLM smoothing)
options.summary: The engine deterministically selects authored epilogue fragments from the run's event ledger; one Sonnet call smooths transitions and connective tissue without adding facts. Cached in the save file.
options.pros: Every run ends with a 'written for me' payoff — the culmination of the memory pillar and the most shareable moment; best latency profile (pre-fetch during final scene); ~$0.04/run; fallback (fragments + authored connectives) is the proven CRPG slideshow pattern and genuinely good.
options.cons: Medium-low tone risk of voice-flattening — needs few-shot style examples, low temperature, and a post-check that fragment key-phrases survive; fragment library must be designed with standard entry/exit shapes, which constrains epilogue authoring; failure at the emotional climax is costly, so pre-fetch + cached fallback is mandatory, not optional.

options.name: 4. LLM-voiced character (constrained free dialogue)
options.summary: One character (ideally diegetically uncanny) is freely conversable, driven by a character bible + ledger-derived known facts in the system prompt.
options.pros: The most ambitious 'the game is alive' feeling; unbounded conversational breadth no dialogue tree can match; ledger-conditioned knowledge makes memory tangible; caching keeps Haiku cost to ~$0.10–0.30/run.
options.cons: Highest risk to both core pillars: tone drift undermines the authored-voice cast, and proximity to the twist creates a spoiler/fairness surface; every line has network latency in an otherwise-instant game; unbounded cost by design; the deterministic fallback is a full authored dialogue tree, meaning you write the character anyway and the LLM buys breadth at maximal risk. Not recommended as specified — fold into a single bounded dream encounter.

options.name: 5. LLM dream sequences (unreliable flavor text)
options.summary: Between chapters, short non-interactive dream/static sequences generate 100–200 words from ledger events + an authored image vocabulary, framed explicitly as corrupted/unreliable signal.
options.pros: The 'unreliable' frame absorbs LLM weirdness diegetically; ~$0.02/run; fully pre-fetchable (zero visible latency); safe home for touchpoint-4 ambition via one lightly interactive dream using the touchpoint-2 classifier; fallback pool with slot substitution is shippable.
options.cons: Lowest emotional ceiling of the five — risk is blandness, not breakage; needs a hard vocabulary constraint (no new proper nouns) to avoid accidental lore/spoiler implications; adds a fourth prompt template to maintain for modest payoff; easiest to cut, which also means easiest to never miss.

topPick: Ship touchpoints 1 + 2 + 3 together — the character-creation interview, the say-anything classifier moments, and the LLM-smoothed personalized epilogue. They form a single coherent emotional arc (the game asks who you are, hears your own words at the crisis, and writes you a personal goodbye), cost ~$0.07–0.10 per full playthrough on Haiku/Sonnet, carry near-zero tone risk because the LLM either does silent extraction/classification or paraphrases authored prose, and every one has a deterministic fallback strong enough to BE the shipped no-API-key game. Architecture: a three-driver AIAdapter in the engine core (none / bring-your-own-key / tiny edge proxy holding the prompts server-side), with the golden rule that the LLM reads the event ledger but never writes canonical state. Add dreams (5) as a stretch; do not build the persistent LLM-voiced character (4) — it threatens the authored-voice and fair-mystery pillars the game depends on.
risks: 1) Who pays: proxy mode means the creator funds all web players (~$0.10/run — fine at indie scale but needs a monthly spend kill-switch that degrades gracefully to deterministic mode); BYOK shifts cost but adds onboarding friction — decide the default. 2) Fallback quality bar: the no-key game must be a complete, good game, not a demo — this roughly doubles authoring for touchpoints 1–3 and the creator must commit to it. 3) Determinism policy: decide now that LLM outputs are cached in save files and never regenerated, or replays/ending-galleries will subtly diverge. 4) Classifier miss at a climactic beat is the single worst failure mode — needs playtesting with real free-text corpora and a confidence-threshold-to-menu fallback. 5) Player-text safety: personal details and echoed player words need profanity/PII filtering, and an explicit policy (ideally an authored in-fiction branch) for abusive input. 6) Spoiler hygiene: enforce a hard rule that no prompt template ever contains twist-adjacent facts — the mystery's fairness depends on it. 7) Pricing/model drift: costs assume Haiku 4.5 ($1/$5 per MTok) and Sonnet 4.6 ($3/$15) as of mid-2026; pin model IDs and re-test tone on any model upgrade. 8) Open question: whether the creator wants any touchpoint-4-style 'living' entity at all — if yes, scope it as one bounded, uncanny dream encounter and prototype the jailbreak-deflection experience early."
